content.text: Branching, evaluating, and backtracking reasoning paths is the key idea behind
Tree-of-Thoughts

(ToT) — a prompting framework that lifts language model reasoning from a single linear chain into a structured search over a tree of intermediate thoughts. Unlike standard chain-of-thought prompting, which commits to one sequence of reasoning steps, ToT lets an orchestrator generate multiple candidate thoughts at each decision point, score them, keep only the most promising, and retry from better states if a path fails.
What it is

Tree-of-Thoughts is a prompting and orchestration framework introduced by Yao et al. (2023) in "Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models." It models the reasoning process as a search over a tree where each node is a
thought
— a coherent, partial solution or reasoning step — and each edge represents the model generating a continuation from the parent thought.
The orchestrator controls four operations: (1)
propose
, generating several candidate next thoughts from the current state; (2)
evaluate
, scoring each thought for progress toward the goal; (3)
select
, keeping the k best-scoring thoughts and pruning the rest; and (4)
backtrack

, returning to a higher-scoring parent when a branch hits a dead end. These four primitives can be composed under any classical search strategy — breadth-first search (BFS), depth-first search (DFS), or beam search.

Crucially, both the propose step and the evaluate step use the language model itself. The propose prompt asks the model to generate N candidate next steps. The evaluate prompt asks the model (or a separate critic model) to rate each candidate on a heuristic such as feasibility, progress, or partial correctness. The orchestrator is pure Python or any host language; no fine-tuning is required.
Why it matters

Standard chain-of-thought prompting is greedy — the model picks one next token at a time without any look-ahead. For problems that require exploration or where early mistakes compound, a single chain often fails silently: the model generates a confident answer that is wrong because it took a bad first step from which there was no recovery.

ToT addresses this fundamental limitation by making the search explicit and separable from the model's generation. Problems that benefit from exploration — math puzzles, code generation with tests, multi-step planning, crossword solving, creative writing with structural constraints — become tractable because the orchestrator can restart from any non-pruned node. This mirrors how a human expert tackles hard problems: they jot down several approaches, realize one is a dead end, cross it off, and continue from a more promising sketch.

For agentic systems, ToT is especially valuable when a task has a verifiable reward signal (test-suite pass rate, constraint satisfaction, etc.) that can act as the evaluator. Rather than asking a human to validate partial plans, the orchestrator runs a lightweight check and prunes automatically, enabling fully autonomous multi-step reasoning at higher accuracy than any single-shot prompt can achieve.
Benefits
Higher accuracy on combinatorial problems.
Yao et al. reported that ToT solved 74% of Game of 24 puzzles versus 4% for standard CoT.
Backtracking prevents error compounding.
When a reasoning path reaches a dead end, the orchestrator backtracks to the last scored node rather than propagating the error all the way to the final answer.
Model-agnostic.
The propose and evaluate prompts are model-agnostic; any instruction-following LLM can fill both roles. The search logic lives entirely in host code.
Parallelisable exploration.
Multiple branches can be expanded and evaluated concurrently, using async LLM calls to reduce wall-clock latency.
Interpretable reasoning trace.
The full search tree is available as a data structure, making it possible to inspect every path explored, every dead end pruned, and the final winning path — useful for debugging and auditing.
Trade-offs
Token cost multiplied by branching factor.
Generating k thoughts per node and d levels deep costs O(k^d) LLM calls in the worst case for DFS and O(k * b^d) for BFS with beam width b. For large trees this becomes prohibitively expensive.
Latency scales with tree depth.
Each level requires at least one serial propose-then-evaluate round. Even with parallelism within a level, a 5-level tree with 3 thoughts per level takes at least 5 serial LLM round-trips.
Evaluator quality bottleneck.

The search is only as good as the scoring heuristic. A weak evaluator sends the orchestrator down low-quality branches and prunes good ones. Designing a reliable prompt-based evaluator is often the hardest part.
Orchestration complexity.
ToT requires non-trivial host code for the search loop, state management, tree tracking, and partial-state serialization. This is significantly more complex than a simple prompt chain.
Overkill for simple tasks.
For tasks where chain-of-thought already achieves high accuracy, ToT adds latency and cost with no benefit. The technique's value is concentrated at the hard end of the difficulty spectrum.
How it works
The ToT framework decomposes a problem into a series of
thought steps
, where each step is a natural-language passage that represents one unit of reasoning. The number of thoughts per step and the search strategy are hyperparameters chosen per task.
Thought generation (propose)

Given the current state — the problem statement plus all ancestor thoughts on the current path — the orchestrator calls the model with a propose prompt that requests exactly k candidate next thoughts. Two prompt styles exist:
Sample-based:
Call the model k times independently at temperature > 0. Good for diverse exploration.
Propose-all-at-once:
Ask the model to list k distinct thoughts in a single call. More token-efficient but the thoughts are correlated.
Thought evaluation (score)
Each generated thought is scored with an evaluate prompt. Two evaluation modes are used:
Value:
The model outputs a score (sure / likely / impossible, or a numeric rating from 1–10) for each thought independently. Suitable when thoughts can be assessed in isolation.
Vote:
The model is shown all k thoughts and asked to vote for the best one. Useful when relative quality is easier to judge than absolute quality.
Search strategy

BFS keeps the b best nodes at each depth level and expands all of them in parallel before moving deeper. DFS fully explores one branch until it terminates or hits a depth limit, then backtracks. Beam search is BFS with a fixed beam width: only the top-b nodes survive each level. Most practical ToT implementations use beam search because it bounds cost while still exploring multiple branches.
The state space size is bounded by:
where k is the branching factor (thoughts per node) and d is the maximum depth. In practice, pruning reduces the explored states to a small fraction of this bound.
Termination

The search terminates when a leaf node satisfies the success criterion (e.g., solves the puzzle, passes tests), when the budget (max LLM calls or max tokens) is exhausted, or when all active branches are marked as dead ends. If no solution is found within budget, the highest-scoring partial solution is returned.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the ToT search tree: the orchestrator generates k thoughts per node, scores each, prunes low-scoring branches (red, dashed), and continues expanding the best paths until a solution node (green) is found.

attrs.alt: Tree-of-Thoughts architecture: search tree with propose, evaluate, select, and backtrack phases

content.text: The orchestrator layer (right panel) drives the four primitives in a loop. The search strategy (BFS / DFS / beam) and budget constraints are configured once and apply uniformly across all levels. The LLM sees only the path from root to the current node — the rest of the tree is maintained in the orchestrator's data structure, invisible to the model.
When to use it
Good fits
Problems with a verifiable partial-progress signal (game state, constraint satisfaction, test pass rate).
Tasks where the optimal answer requires exploring multiple incompatible first steps (mathematical proofs, planning).
Multi-step code generation where unit tests can evaluate each intermediate state.
Creative writing with hard structural constraints (e.g., crossword clues, acrostic poems).
Agentic planning where intermediate sub-goals need to be validated before committing to a path.
Poor fits
Tasks that chain-of-thought already solves reliably — the overhead is unjustified.
Latency-sensitive applications where a response is needed in under 2 seconds.
Open-ended generation without a measurable quality signal — the evaluator becomes arbitrary.
Very long contexts where serializing the full path to each node overflows the context window.

A practical decision heuristic: if a single-shot CoT attempt fails more than 20–30% of the time on evaluation data and the task has a verifiable criterion, ToT is worth the added complexity. If single-shot already achieves >80% accuracy, the marginal gain from ToT is unlikely to justify the cost.
How to implement

A minimal ToT implementation requires four components: a state node that tracks the path from root, a propose function that calls the LLM for k candidate next thoughts, an evaluate function that scores each candidate, and a beam-search loop that ties them together. The following walkthrough uses Python with the OpenAI SDK; the same structure applies to any LLM client.
State, propose, and evaluate
Beam search loop

import heapq

def tot_beam(problem: str, max_depth: int = 4, beam_width: int = 3, k: int = 3) -> str:
    root = ThoughtNode(thought="(start)", score=10.0, depth=0)
    frontier = [(-root.score, root)]

    for depth in range(1, max_depth + 1):
        next_frontier = []
        for _, node in frontier:
            thoughts = propose(problem, node, k=k)
            scores = evaluate(problem, node, thoughts)
            for thought, score in zip(thoughts, scores):
                child = ThoughtNode(thought=thought, score=score, depth=depth, parent=node)
                node.children.append(child)
                heapq.heappush(next_frontier, (-score, child))
        frontier = heapq.nsmallest(beam_width, next_frontier)

    best = frontier[0][1]
    return "\n".join(f"Step {i+1}: {t}" for i, t in enumerate(best.path()[1:]))

problem = "Find a 4-digit number where the sum of digits equals 20 and it is divisible by 11."
print(tot_beam(problem, max_depth=3, beam_width=2, k=3))
Examples
Example 1 — Game of 24 (introductory)

The Game of 24 asks for a mathematical expression using four numbers (each exactly once) that evaluates to 24. This is the canonical ToT benchmark. The following self-contained implementation uses Python with mocked LLM calls to illustrate the mechanics without requiring an API key.

"""
Simplified ToT for Game of 24.
Replace mock_llm with a real LLM client for production use.
"""
import itertools, operator

def mock_llm_propose(numbers: list[int]) -> list[str]:
    """Generate candidate arithmetic expressions over numbers."""
    ops = ['+', '-', '*', '/']
    candidates = []
    for perm in itertools.permutations(numbers, len(numbers)):
        for op1, op2, op3 in itertools.product(ops, repeat=3):
            expr = f"(({perm[0]} {op1} {perm[1]}) {op2} {perm[2]}) {op3} {perm[3]}"
            candidates.append(expr)
    return candidates[:10]  # return top 10 for demo

def mock_llm_evaluate(expressions: list[str]) -> list[float]:
    """Score each expression by how close it evaluates to 24."""
    scores = []
    for expr in expressions:
        try:
            val = eval(expr)  # safe here; real code should use ast.literal_eval or similar
            scores.append(10.0 if abs(val - 24) < 1e-6 else max(0.0, 10.0 - abs(val - 24)))
        except (ZeroDivisionError, SyntaxError):
            scores.append(0.0)
    return scores

def game_of_24_tot(numbers: list[int]) -> str:
    candidates = mock_llm_propose(numbers)
    scores = mock_llm_evaluate(candidates)
    ranked = sorted(zip(scores, candidates), reverse=True)
    best_score, best_expr = ranked[0]
    if best_score == 10.0:
        return f"Solution: {best_expr} = 24"
    return f"Best attempt: {best_expr} ≈ {eval(best_expr):.2f}"

print(game_of_24_tot([4, 6, 8, 2]))
# Solution: ((4 * 6) / 8) * ... (one valid expression)
Example 2 — Multi-step code generation with test-based evaluation

This realistic example uses ToT to generate Python code for a problem where correctness is verified by running unit tests. Each thought is a code snippet; the evaluator runs the tests and returns a pass-rate score.

import subprocess, tempfile, os, textwrap
import openai

client = openai.OpenAI()

PROBLEM = """
Write a Python function `merge_intervals(intervals)` that takes a list of
[start, end] pairs and returns a list of merged non-overlapping intervals,
sorted by start time.
"""

TESTS = """
def test_merge():
    assert merge_intervals([[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]]) == [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]]
    assert merge_intervals([[1,4],[4,5]]) == [[1,5]]
    assert merge_intervals([]) == []
    assert merge_intervals([[1,1]]) == [[1,1]]
    print("All tests passed.")

test_merge()
"""

def run_tests(code_snippet: str) -> float:
    """Return fraction of tests passed (0.0–1.0)."""
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(mode='w', suffix='.py', delete=False) as f:
        f.write(code_snippet + "\n" + TESTS)
        fname = f.name
    try:
        result = subprocess.run(
            ["python3", fname], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5
        )
        return 1.0 if "All tests passed" in result.stdout else 0.0
    except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
        return 0.0
    finally:
        os.unlink(fname)

def propose_code(problem: str, previous: str, k: int = 3) -> list[str]:
    prompt = (
        f"Problem: {problem}\n\n"
        f"Previous attempt:\n{previous}\n\n"
        f"Generate {k} improved Python implementations of the function. "
        "Output each as a complete function, separated by '---'."
    )
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        temperature=0.6,
    )
    raw = resp.choices[0].message.content
    parts = [p.strip() for p in raw.split("---") if p.strip()]
    return parts[:k]

def code_tot(problem: str, max_rounds: int = 3, k: int = 3) -> str:
    current_best = ("", 0.0)
    for round_num in range(max_rounds):
        candidates = propose_code(problem, current_best[0], k=k)
        for code in candidates:
            score = run_tests(code)
            if score > current_best[1]:
                current_best = (code, score)
            if score == 1.0:
                return code  # perfect solution found
    return current_best[0]

solution = code_tot(PROBLEM)
print(solution)
Real-world example

A software engineering team uses ToT to generate and debug multi-function Python modules against a CI test suite. The workflow runs inside a Docker container where each code candidate is executed in an isolated subprocess with a 5-second timeout, and the pass rate from pytest is used as the ToT score.

The orchestrator runs 3 rounds of beam search with beam width 2 and branching factor 3, totalling at most 3 × 2 × 3 = 18 LLM calls per task. In practice, successful tasks converge in round 1 or 2. Tasks that fail all 18 calls fall back to a human review queue rather than shipping broken code.

import asyncio, openai

client = openai.AsyncOpenAI()

async def async_beam_level(problem: str, frontier: list[dict], k: int) -> list[dict]:
    """Expand one BFS level with fully-parallelised propose + evaluate calls."""
    # Propose k thoughts for every frontier node concurrently
    propose_resps = await asyncio.gather(*[
        client.chat.completions.create(
            model="gpt-4o", temperature=0.7,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
                f"Problem: {problem}\nPath: {node['path']}\n"
                f"List {k} distinct next steps, one per line."}],
        )
        for node in frontier
    ])
    all_children = []
    for node, resp in zip(frontier, propose_resps):
        thoughts = [ln.strip() for ln in resp.choices[0].message.content.splitlines() if ln.strip()][:k]
        for t in thoughts:
            all_children.append({"path": node["path"] + [t], "score": 0.0, "thought": t})

    # Evaluate all children concurrently
    eval_resps = await asyncio.gather(*[
        client.chat.completions.create(
            model="gpt-4o", temperature=0.0,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
                f"Problem: {problem}\nPath: {c['path']}\nScore 0-10 (integer only):"}],
        )
        for c in all_children
    ])
    for child, resp in zip(all_children, eval_resps):
        try:
            child["score"] = float(resp.choices[0].message.content.strip().split()[0])
        except (ValueError, IndexError):
            child["score"] = 0.0
    return all_children

async def async_tot(problem: str, depth: int = 3, beam: int = 2, k: int = 3) -> list[str]:
    frontier = [{"path": [], "score": 10.0}]
    for _ in range(depth):
        children = await async_beam_level(problem, frontier, k)
        children.sort(key=lambda x: x["score"], reverse=True)
        frontier = children[:beam]
    return frontier[0]["path"]

# asyncio.run(async_tot("Implement a thread-safe LRU cache in Python"))

All propose and evaluate calls within each level are issued concurrently via asyncio.gather. This means a 3-level, beam-2, k-3 search (18 total LLM calls) completes in roughly 3 serial round-trips instead of 18, reducing wall-clock time from ~36 s to ~8–10 s at typical API latencies.
Common mistakes
Mistake 1 — Evaluator that parrots the proposer

Using the same prompt for both propose and evaluate leads the model to rate the thoughts it just generated as high-quality regardless of actual merit. The evaluator must be independent — either a separate model call with a different, critic-oriented prompt, or a programmatic check (test runner, constraint solver).

# WRONG: propose and evaluate share the same framing
def bad_evaluate(thought: str) -> float:
    prompt = f"Is this a good next step? {thought}"  # too open-ended; model always says yes
    ...

# RIGHT: evaluator uses a critical, structured rubric
def good_evaluate(problem: str, path: list[str], thought: str) -> float:
    prompt = (
        f"Problem: {problem}\n"
        f"Path so far: {path}\n"
        f"Proposed step: {thought}\n\n"
        "Does this step move toward the solution or introduce an error? "
        "Output ONLY one of: sure / likely / unlikely / impossible"
    )
    ...
Mistake 2 — Unlimited branching depth
Forgetting to cap both depth and beam width causes exponential token usage. Always set explicit limits before deploying a ToT pipeline.
Best practices
Design the evaluator first.
If you cannot write a reliable scoring function, ToT will not improve results. Use programmatic checks (tests, constraint solvers) whenever possible; rely on LLM-based scoring only as a fallback.
Start with beam width 2–3 and depth 3–4.
Widen only if evals show that the solution is consistently on a pruned branch.
Parallelise within each level.
Use async calls so all k proposals and their evaluations within a level happen concurrently. This keeps wall-clock latency proportional to depth, not to total LLM calls.
Cache evaluate results.
If the same thought appears on multiple paths (possible in BFS), cache the score by (path, thought) hash to avoid redundant LLM calls.
Log the full search tree.
Store every node, score, and pruning decision. This data is invaluable for tuning the propose and evaluate prompts.
Fail gracefully.
Return the best partial solution rather than raising an exception when the budget is exhausted. Let the caller decide whether to accept a partial answer or escalate to a human.
Performance & scalability
The computational complexity of ToT scales with the chosen search strategy. For beam search, the total LLM call count is:

where d is depth, b is beam width, and k is branching factor. A configuration of d=4, b=3, k=3 requires at most 72 LLM calls. At 2 seconds per call sequentially, that is 144 seconds; with full parallelism within levels, it reduces to 4 × (3 + 3) × 2 s ÷ parallelism ≈ 24 seconds if each level is fully parallelised.
Latency optimisation
Use async LLM calls to parallelise all nodes within a level.
Use a smaller, faster model for the evaluate step and reserve the large model for propose.
Apply early termination: stop the search immediately when a node scores 10/10 (provably correct).
Reduce k (branching factor) before reducing b (beam width) — fewer candidates with more survivors is usually better than many candidates with few survivors.
Token cost optimisation
Use prompt caching on the shared prefix (problem statement) to save input tokens on every call.
Truncate the path serialisation to the last N steps when context limits are tight.
Consider a two-tier evaluator: a cheap heuristic first (regex or rule-based), then LLM scoring only for borderline cases.
Tooling & ecosystem
LangGraph

LangGraph (part of the LangChain ecosystem) provides a graph execution framework that natively models branching, backtracking, and conditional routing. A ToT implementation in LangGraph defines each thought node as a graph node and the evaluate-and-select logic as a conditional edge.

from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from typing import TypedDict

class ToTState(TypedDict):
    problem: str
    path: list[str]
    depth: int
    scores: list[float]

def propose_node(state: ToTState) -> ToTState:
    # call LLM to generate thoughts
    thoughts = propose(state["problem"], state["path"], k=3)
    state["thoughts"] = thoughts
    return state

def evaluate_node(state: ToTState) -> ToTState:
    scores = evaluate(state["problem"], state["path"], state["thoughts"])
    state["scores"] = scores
    return state

def select_node(state: ToTState) -> str:
    best_score = max(state["scores"])
    if best_score >= 9.0 or state["depth"] >= 4:
        return END
    return "propose"

graph = StateGraph(ToTState)
graph.add_node("propose", propose_node)
graph.add_node("evaluate", evaluate_node)
graph.add_conditional_edges("evaluate", select_node)
graph.add_edge("propose", "evaluate")
graph.set_entry_point("propose")
app = graph.compile()
Microsoft Guidance

Microsoft Guidance is a templating library that gives fine-grained control over LLM output tokens, making it easy to constrain the propose step to exactly k numbered thoughts and the evaluate step to a strict numeric output — no post-processing required.
DSPy

DSPy (Stanford) frames LLM pipelines as differentiable programs. A ToT propose-evaluate loop can be expressed as a DSPy module, then auto-optimised with the MIPRO or BootstrapFewShot optimiser to improve the propose and evaluate prompts using labelled examples — useful when gold-standard solutions are available for a domain and prompt tuning would otherwise require manual iteration.
Comparison with alternatives
Technique
Reasoning structure
LLM calls
Backtracking
Best for
Chain-of-Thought (CoT)
Single linear chain
No
Simple step-by-step reasoning
Self-Consistency (SC)
k independent chains, majority vote
k (parallel)
No
Tasks with deterministic correct answers
Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT)
Search tree with propose/evaluate/prune
O(k × d × b)
Yes
Hard planning, combinatorial problems
Algorithm-of-Thoughts (AoT)
Algorithmic search pattern in one call
1–few
Implicit (in-context)
Moderate-complexity search within one context window
Least-to-Most
Decompose then solve in order
1 per sub-task
No
Tasks with clear sub-task decomposition
MCTS + LLM
Monte Carlo rollouts on LLM tree
O(rollouts)
Yes
Game-like problems with simulation budget

ToT occupies the niche where problem complexity justifies multi-call search but a simulator (as in MCTS) is not available. When a fast simulator exists, MCTS typically outperforms ToT. When the problem decomposes cleanly into ordered sub-tasks, Least-to-Most is simpler and cheaper.
Security & reliability
Prompt injection in thought nodes

Because the path (sequence of thoughts) is concatenated into each subsequent prompt, an adversarial thought inserted early in the tree can influence all downstream calls. For agentic systems that use ToT to plan external actions (file writes, API calls), validate and sanitise each thought before including it in the path or before executing any action it describes.
Evaluator gaming

A sufficiently capable model may learn to generate thoughts that score well on the evaluator prompt without actually being correct — analogous to reward hacking in reinforcement learning. Mitigation strategies include using a separate, smaller evaluator model that is harder to game, using programmatic evaluation (tests, formal checks) rather than LLM scoring, and periodically auditing the search tree for systematically high-scoring but wrong paths.
Cost runaway

Without a hard token budget, a misconfigured ToT loop can exhaust an API quota rapidly. Always wrap the search loop in a try/finally block that logs the total tokens consumed, and set a per-task token ceiling in addition to the depth and beam-width limits.

MAX_TOKENS_PER_TASK = 100_000

def safe_tot(problem: str) -> str:
    tokens_used = 0
    # Patch the client to accumulate token counts
    ...
    try:
        result = tot_bfs(problem)
    except TokenBudgetExceeded:
        result = "(budget exhausted — returning best partial solution)"
    finally:
        print(f"Tokens used: {tokens_used}")
    return result
Reliability under model variability

LLM output is stochastic. The same propose prompt may return 2 or 4 thoughts instead of the requested 3. Always add a parsing layer that normalises and pads/truncates the output to the expected length. Treat unexpected output formats as low-scoring rather than raising exceptions.
Key takeaways
Tree-of-Thoughts lifts LLM reasoning from a single greedy chain into a structured search tree, enabling backtracking and multi-path exploration.
The four core primitives — propose, evaluate, select, and backtrack — are orchestrated by host code; the LLM handles only the propose and evaluate prompts.
Beam search with depth 3–4 and width 2–3 is the practical default: it bounds cost while covering most of the solution space.
The quality of the evaluate step determines overall performance; use programmatic checks (tests, constraint solvers) in preference to LLM self-rating.
Parallelising LLM calls within each tree level is essential for keeping latency manageable at reasonable beam widths.
ToT is most valuable when single-pass CoT fails >20% of the time and a verifiable progress signal exists; it is overkill for simpler tasks.
Always cap token consumption with explicit depth, beam-width, and budget limits, and log the full search tree for debugging.
LangGraph, DSPy, and Microsoft Guidance all provide first-class building blocks for assembling production-grade ToT pipelines.
